column5: Chicken or the fish? No, genuinely, what do you think? I love that we decide everything together. I hate doing stuff without you.
column6: He likes us to decide most things together, even small ones, and calls it being a team. It's sweet and inclusive, though I've started noticing he genuinely can't decide anything without me.
column7: Seeded from Neediness band-8 ('can't choose lunch without deciding together'): honeymoon faint, the togetherness reads as team-sweetness; the dependence ('struggles without me') is the faint seam. Residue (his decisions becoming hers) dormant.

column4: just to hear your voice (Neediness8 faint)
column5: Sorry, I know you're at work. I just missed your voice. Five minutes. Okay, go, go be brilliant.
column6: He'll call during my workday just to hear my voice, says it settles him. It's flattering to be missed that much, and he's so sweet about it, though I do feel a little tug to wrap things up and get back to him.
column7: Seeded from Neediness band-8 ('call repeatedly just to hear my voice and calm their anxiety'): honeymoon faint, the calls read as devotion; her cost (the 'tug' to prioritize him) is the seam. Residue (her attention reorganizing around his need) dormant.

column5: You've been quiet, are we okay? You're the best thing in my life and I get scared I'll do something to lose it.
column6: When I'm quiet he worries we're not okay and needs a little reassurance, then he's fine. It's tender, this fear of losing what we have, though it does mean my quiet moods come with reassuring him on the side.
column7: Seeded from Attachment band-8 ('Do you still love me? You've been quiet all day'): honeymoon faint, the reassurance-need reads as tender; her cost (reassuring him through her own off-moods) is the seam. NOT yet the smothered/eggshells full version. Residue (caretaker of his security) dormant.

column5: What do you think I should do? You're so much better at this than me. Just tell me what you'd do and I'll do that.
column6: He leans on me for nearly every decision because he says I'm so much better at it, which was flattering at first. Now I'm quietly making choices for both of us all day long, and it's a weight I didn't sign up for and can't seem to put down.
column7: Neediness8 'can't decide without me.' Targets overwhelmed (deciding for two) + resentful (didn't sign up for it). Honeymoon: started as flattering.

column4: I end up finishing it (transferred load)
column5: You're so much better at all the bills and calls and planning. My brain doesn't work that way. I'd be lost without you.
column6: Somehow I've become the one who handles the bills, the appointments, the planning, all of it, while he cheers me on as his hero. The praise is warm but the load is all mine, and I've started feeling more like his manager than his partner.
column7: Neediness/Adulthood: load transfer framed as praise. Targets overwhelmed (all the load) + resentful (manager not partner) + lonely. Honeymoon-faint.

column5: I made you tea! In bed! It's slightly wrong but I tried, you know I'm useless at this. Look at me taking care of you for once.
column6: He makes these small sweet attempts to take care of me and is so proud of them that I always praise him, and I want to. But I've noticed a cup of tea from him is treated like a huge deal, while everything I do just gets taken for granted.
column7: Effort-as-Heroic-Achievement. Targets resentful (asymmetric bar) + overwhelmed (her work absorbed) + lonely. Honeymoon: genuinely endearing.

column5: Did you get my texts? Okay, more than a few. I just think of things I want to tell you all day. Is that too much?
column6: He texts me constantly through the day and gets a little anxious if I'm slow to reply, and I've started feeling tethered to my phone, managing his worry from afar. It's affectionate, but it's a lot, and I miss just being unreachable sometimes.
column7: Neediness8 'text 20+ times, panic if no reply.' Targets overwhelmed (tethered) + resentful (miss being unreachable) + lonely. Honeymoon-faint.

column6: Being adored like this is something I never had before, and I don't want to sound ungrateful for it. But I've quietly become the one who runs everything, soothes everything, decides everything, while being told I'm wonderful for it. I'm carrying two lives, I feel oddly alone inside all that devotion, and some days I'm just tired and a little resentful, which then makes me feel guilty.
column7: Residue. Targets all three: overwhelmed (carrying two lives), lonely (alone inside the devotion), resentful (tired of it). Names no tactic.

column5: I'm a real softie, I feel things deeply.
column6: He seems sweet and gentle, not hard or intimidating.
column7: His softness can become emotional dependency she must manage.

column5: I'm a giver, I just want someone to take care of.
column6: He sounds nurturing, like he loves making someone feel cared for.
column7: Caretaking may be a bid to become needed while avoiding adult self-management.

column5: I'm happiest when I have someone to build my world around.
column6: It feels flattering, like I could matter deeply in his life.
column7: He lacks a stable independent center; she becomes the organizing structure.

column5: I don't really function well on my own.
column6: It sounds vulnerable and honest, like he trusts me with his weakness.
column7: He is advertising helplessness; she will be pulled into managing his life.

column5: I'm not practical, but I have a huge heart.
column6: He sounds lovable and emotionally rich despite being messy.
column7: Affection is offered as compensation for practical incompetence she must absorb.

column5: I need someone steady around me to be my best.
column6: It feels meaningful, like my stability helps him grow.
column7: His functioning is outsourced to her presence and support.

column5: I'm better at dreams than details.
column6: He sounds creative and sweet, not boring or rigid.
column7: Details will fall to her while he keeps the dreamer role.

column5: I'm the kind of guy who needs a little saving.
column6: It sounds self-aware and endearing, like he knows he's messy.
column7: Saving becomes her role; leaving feels like abandoning someone in need.

column5: I love being taken care of, I won't pretend I don't.
column6: He sounds honest and sweetly open about receiving love.
column7: Being taken care of may become a one-way dependency.

column5: I need someone to lean on, and I'll adore whoever lets me.
column6: He sounds gentle and comforting, like he would be easy to love.
column7: He may offer emotional sweetness while relying on her for structure and responsibility.

column5: I do best when someone believes in me.
column6: It feels beautiful that my faith could help him shine.
column7: Belief becomes labor she must provide, even when behavior doesn't change.

column5: I'm very loyal to whoever helps me feel safe.
column6: It sounds tender, like he bonds through trust.
column7: Safety becomes something she must supply for his loyalty to stay intact.

column5: I attach to people who see the good in me.
column6: It feels special to be someone who sees him clearly.
column7: He attaches to affirmation; she must keep seeing good to maintain him.

column5: I'm a little lost, but in a hopeful way.
column6: He sounds sweet and open, like he just needs direction.
column7: Lostness becomes her project, not his responsibility.

column5: I need a partner who can ground me.
column6: It feels flattering that he sees me as steady and capable.
column7: She is being cast as regulator and stabilizer.

column5: I'm happiest when I can lean into someone.
column6: It sounds intimate and trusting.
column7: Leaning can become chronic dependence without reciprocity.

column5: I'm not ashamed to need help.
column6: He sounds emotionally mature and honest.
column7: Needing help can become an identity that exempts him from building capacity.

column5: I'm a heart-first person, logistics come later.
column6: He sounds romantic and emotionally led.
column7: Logistics coming later means she handles the reality while he keeps the feeling.

column5: I love hard when someone makes me feel capable.
column6: It feels powerful that I can bring out his best self.
column7: His capability depends on her emotional support, making her responsible for his functioning.

column5: I want a nurturing woman who likes taking care of people.
column6: He appreciates my caring nature and values a sweet supportive partner.
column7: He is selecting for a maternal surrogate who will manage his life while he evades adult responsibility.

column5: I need a strong partner who can keep me grounded.
column6: He admires my strength and capability, looking for a powerful woman to walk beside.
column7: He is planning to outsource all major financial, logistical, and emotional burdens to her.

column5: I'm looking for someone who loves to look after someone.
column6: He is sweet and ready to be loved and cared for by a generous woman.
column7: He is filtering for an over-functioning caretaker who will tolerate his chronic under-functioning.

column5: I want a woman who can help me organize my chaotic life.
column6: He is a scattered creative who respects my organizational skills.
column7: He is looking for an unpaid personal assistant to handle his practical failures.

column5: I need a partner who can be my rock when everything falls apart.
column6: He is vulnerable and knows he needs a stable strong woman during storms.
column7: He is preparing to unleash a continuous series of self-made crises for her to solve.

column5: I like a girl who takes charge and knows what she wants.
column6: He loves a confident empowered woman, he doesn't have a fragile ego.
column7: He is setting a trap where she must make every difficult decision, letting him blame her for failures.

column5: I want someone who can guide me toward being a better man.
column6: He is humble and wants a wise woman to help him grow.
column7: He is forcing her into the role of a life coach, shifting blame for his stagnation onto her coaching.

column5: I need a woman who handles all the details beautifully.
column6: He notices my organizational skills and appreciates how competent I am.
column7: He is planning to dump all domestic and financial labor entirely onto her shoulders.

column5: I'm looking for a partner who is comfortable being the stable anchor.
column6: He values my steady career and grounded personality, which feels validating.
column7: He is screening for a target he can financially depend on while he avoids steady employment.

column5: I want a compassionate soul who doesn't mind helping a man rebuild.
column6: He is open about his past failures and looking for a loving woman to support his comeback.
column7: He is seeking a target who will fund his lifestyle and excuse his lack of basic adult skills.

column5: I need someone who can handle a guy who is a bit of a project.
column6: He is self-aware and charmingly honest about needing some love and refinement.
column7: He is framing his deep behavioral issues as a cute project for her to fix.

column5: I like a partner who naturally takes care of the home.
column6: He values traditional home comfort and loves my homemaking skills.
column7: He is ensuring he will never have to contribute to chores or domestic labor.

column5: I want a highly responsible woman who has her life together.
column6: He respects my maturity and stability, which makes me feel accomplished.
column7: He is hunting for a safety net to protect him from the consequences of his reckless behavior.

column5: I need a partner who doesn't mind giving me a push sometimes.
column6: He wants a motivating teammate who will help him stay focused on his goals.
column7: He is installing her as his external discipline engine, making her bear the cost of his laziness.

column5: I'm looking for a woman who can hold things down while I pursue my dreams.
column6: He is passionate about his goals and looking for a supportive believing partner.
column7: He is seeking long-term financial extraction, using his dreams to justify parasitic behavior.

column5: I want someone who can manage the heavy financial planning for us.
column6: He trusts my financial intelligence and wants me to lead on our future.
column7: He is positioning himself to spend freely while she handles the stressful burden of budgeting.

column5: I need a girl who is patient with my emotional ups and downs.
column6: He is a deep emotional person who needs a steady calm partner to soothe him. He is looking for someone who will keep him calm and absorb his moods.

column5: I like a woman who enjoys making sure I'm okay.
column6: He finds sweet practical expressions of love endearing and feels cared for.
column7: He is establishing a baseline of daily domestic service where she functions as his mother.

column5: I want a partner who can protect me from my own worst impulses.
column6: He is self-aware about his wild side and looking for a steadying woman.
column7: He is abdicating responsibility for his own actions, pre-blaming her when he gives into bad impulses.

column5: I need someone who can smooth over my conflicts with other people.
column6: He is direct and needs a diplomatic kind woman to help him socially.
column7: He is looking for an apologist to clean up the social wreckage caused by his poor behavior.

column5: There she is. My whole day just got better.
column6: He says his whole day got better when I walked in. It feels amazing to matter that much to someone.
column7: He expresses outsized emotional reliance on her arrival. She feels essential, not yet aware this puts her in charge of his emotional state.

column5: I was just thinking about you and smiling. I know that's cheesy. I don't care.
column6: He admits he was just sitting there smiling about me. It's sweet and a little embarrassing for him. I love that I take up that much space in his head.
column7: He admits a private fixation, which feels like pure adoration. The 'cheesy' vulnerability disarms her, making his need endearing rather than burdensome.

column5: I keep looking over at you like you might disappear. I just can't believe it sometimes.
column6: He says he keeps checking I'm still here, like I'm too good to be true. It's such a vulnerable thing to admit. It makes me feel so wanted.
column7: He frames his insecurity as a compliment to her worth, hinting at a need for her to keep reassuring him of her presence.

column5: I've already told my mom about you. She wants to meet you. I hope that's not too fast.
column6: He's already told his mom about me. It feels serious, like he's folding me into his life. It's fast, but it also feels really secure.
column7: He creates seriousness and social integration via family. A low-cost way to create obligation and depth without structural change.

column5: I'm not really used to this. Someone being this present. It's a lot, but it's a good lot.
column6: He says he's not used to someone being this present. It's a little vulnerable, and it makes me want to show him what he's been missing.
column7: He signals a history of emotional scarcity, activating her caretaking instincts. She wants to prove she's different, not noticing she's being recruited to provide the care.

column5: I love watching you handle things. You're so calm. I just sit back and admire it.
column6: He says he admires watching me handle things. It feels like he sees a strength other people miss. I feel capable and seen.
column7: He praises her competence as the admiring audience. She feels powerful, not noticing his admiration is already outsourcing problem-solving to her.

column5: How do you just know the right thing to do? I'd still be standing there lost.
column6: He says he'd be lost without me. It's nice to be seen as the capable one for once.
column7: He elevates normal competence to an extraordinary quality. The flattery implies she'll be the one who knows what to do when decisions need making.

column5: You just have this way of making everything feel simpler. I don't know how you do it.
column6: He says I make everything feel simpler. It's nice to be that person for someone, the one who brings calm.
column7: He credits her with bringing order to his life, gradually transferring responsibility for managing his chaos onto her.

column5: I was stuck on that for an hour and you solved it in two minutes. You're incredible.
column6: He says I solved something he was stuck on. It feels good to be so obviously useful to him.
column7: He frames her solving a problem as exceptional ability. The compliment assigns her the role of problem-solver in the relationship.

column5: You're so much better with people than I am. You just know what to say. I'm always putting my foot in it.
column6: He says I'm better with people. It's true, I'm more socially comfortable. I like that he sees it as a strength.
column7: He praises a skill he lacks, positioning himself inferior in that domain. This quietly assigns her the role of social manager for the couple.

column5: You're so much better at this than I am. I just watch you and try to learn.
column6: He says I'm better at it than him, that he wants to learn from me. It feels like a team where I'm the expert.
column7: He positions himself as student to her expert. The implication is he'll defer to her, and she becomes the default decision-maker.

column5: I made your coffee exactly how you like it. Two sugars, right?
column6: He remembered my coffee order. It's a small thing, but it means he's paying attention. No one's ever really paid attention before.
column7: He demonstrates care through a small thoughtful act. A low-cost way to create intimacy and obligation.

column5: I got you that thing you mentioned. I just happened to see it and thought of you.
column6: He bought me something I mentioned offhand. It's so thoughtful, like he's always listening for ways to make me smile.
column7: He uses an unexpected small gift to demonstrate attentiveness, designed to deepen her sense of being uniquely cared for.

column5: I love that you do that thing with your hair. I can't explain it, it just makes me smile.
column6: He notices a tiny habit of mine and says it makes him smile. It's so specific and sweet. It makes me feel like he really sees me.
column7: He gives specific praise for a small physical habit. The specificity is the hook, making her feel uniquely known.

column5: I know you're exhausted. Just sit down. Let me handle the little things tonight.
column6: He tells me to sit down, that he'll handle the little things. It's so nice to not have to be in charge for once.
column7: He offers to take over minor tasks for one evening, real relief. This frames his help as an occasional gift while ongoing responsibilities quietly shift to her.

column5: I love that you have your own thing. Your friends, your work. I just want to be part of it, not take it over.
column6: He says he loves that I have my own life and just wants to be part of it. It sounds so healthy, not possessive. It makes me trust him more.
column7: He frames his desire for access as respect for her autonomy. The non-possessiveness reads as a green flag, not a setup to slowly become the center of her world.

column5: I just want to be near you. Even if we're both doing our own thing. Just being in the same room is enough.
column6: He says just being in the same room is enough. It makes me feel like I don't have to perform, I just have to exist near him.
column7: He offers low-demand togetherness that feels safe. It's also a way to keep her near without engaging deeply.

column5: I love that you're so good at what you do. It's inspiring to watch someone who's really in their element.
column6: He says I'm inspiring to watch in my element. It's such a specific compliment, like he really sees my work and my passion.
column7: He praises her professional competence, which feels supportive. The admiration is genuine, but it also sets expectations she'll feel bound to meet.

column5: I've been staring at the menu for ten minutes and I'm completely lost. Just order for me. I trust you.
column6: He says he's lost looking at the menu and wants me to order. It's kind of endearing. I like that he trusts my judgment.
column7: He transfers a minor decision to her, framed as trust. The smallest of many decisions she'll eventually make for both of them.

column5: I tried to pull together that thing you mentioned, but I lost track of the timing. It's a bit of a disaster. I wanted so badly to have it right for you.
column6: He tried to do something nice and it fell apart. He's genuinely disappointed in himself, and it makes me want to step in and fix it.
column7: He presents an incompetent but earnest gesture as a failed attempt to please her. She steps in, not noticing the low bar for his effort being set.

column5: I'm terrible at remembering dates. I've already put everything in my calendar. I don't want to mess up with you.
column6: He admits he's bad with dates and shows he's trying. It's sweet that he's working so hard. It makes me want to help him even more.
column7: He admits a weakness with compensatory effort. Endearing, but it implies she'll be the one tracking and reminding him of things long-term.

column5: I've been trying to figure out what to do about this for days. Can I just tell you about it? You always help me see it clearly.
column6: He says he's been stuck for days and wants to tell me because I help him see clearly. It feels good to be his sounding board.
column7: He frames her as his necessary thinking partner. She feels valued for her insight, not noticing she's becoming his default problem-solving resource.

column5: I honestly think I'd have stayed paralyzed on that decision forever if you hadn't stepped in. You just see the solution instantly.
column6: He says I saw the solution when he was paralyzed. It's nice to cut through the noise for him. I like being useful.
column7: He exaggerates his indecision to make her competence seem extraordinary. She feels like a hero, becoming his default decision-maker.

column5: I love that you take care of me. Not in a weird way. You just make sure I'm okay. No one's ever really done that.
column6: He says he loves that I take care of him, that no one's ever done that. It makes me feel like the first person who's seen he needs looking after.
column7: He frames her caretaking as unique and precious. The gratitude obscures the gradual transfer of responsibility for his wellbeing onto her.

column5: You always know what to say. I'm so glad I have you to talk to.
column6: He says I always know what to say. It feels good to be a source of comfort for him, like I'm really helping.
column7: He positions her as his primary source of emotional wisdom, making her responsible for his emotional regulation.

column5: I've just never had someone who actually listens. Who actually wants to know how I'm doing.
column6: He says he's never had someone who actually listens. It breaks my heart a little. I want to be that person for him.
column7: He expresses a history of emotional neglect, activating her caretaking instincts. She wants to fill the void, not noticing she's being recruited.

column5: I don't know what I'd do without you. Probably just keep spinning.
column6: He says he'd keep spinning without me. It feels good to be that necessary to someone. I like that he needs me.
column7: He explicitly states his emotional need for her. She feels essential, not noticing this need will become a recurring demand for attention and reassurance.

column5: My brain's usually spinning, but when I'm with you all that static just quiets down. You have a steadying effect on me.
column6: He says I quiet his spinning brain. It feels so good to bring someone peace.
column7: He positions her as his emotional regulator, claiming her presence quiets his anxiety. She feels needed, not aware she's becoming responsible for his stability.

column5: I was panicking and you just talked me down. I don't know how you stay so calm.
column6: He says I talked him down from a panic. It feels good to be the steady one. I like that he trusts me with his feelings.
column7: She's cast as his emotional regulator. The trust feels intimate but puts her in charge of managing his distress, a pattern that will repeat.

column5: I feel like I can actually breathe when I'm with you. Like I don't have to pretend. I can just be.
column6: He says he can breathe with me, that he doesn't have to perform. It's so vulnerable. It makes me feel like he trusts me completely.
column7: He credits her with letting him be his authentic self. Flattering, but that 'authentic self' is also deeply dependent on her presence.

column5: I keep telling people about you. I'm like a broken record. It's just exciting to have something this good.
column6: He says he can't stop talking about me. It's flattering, like he's so proud to be with me that he has to tell the world.
column7: He broadcasts his investment to his social circle, making the relationship feel public and serious. Her value reinforced by external validation.

column5: I was telling my friend about you and he said I sounded happier. He's right.
column6: He told his friend I make him happier. It's wonderful to be the reason someone is happier. It makes me feel important.
column7: He uses a third party to validate his emotional reliance on her, making it feel objective. His happiness is becoming her responsibility.

column5: Look at the guy at the bar telling the whole room about his fantasy football trade. Incredible. That's a type, isn't it.
column6: He's so funny, dry and quick, he had me crying laughing all night with his little observations about people. Being with someone that clever and witty is honestly intoxicating.
column7: The genuine draw: sharp, dry, witty (Conflict1, charming surface); humor aimed OUTWARD at the world here, pure good company. The same observational edge that later barbs her is, early, just hilarious. She reads clever/intoxicating. The faint seam (the wit has teeth) dormant.

column5: I'm not going to make a thing of it, life's too short. You want Thai, we get Thai. I'm easy.
column6: He's so easygoing and never makes anything a fight, plus he makes me laugh while he's at it. After exes who turned everything into a battle, being with someone this low-drama and funny is such a relief.
column7: Compliance-as-Surface-Veneer (Conflict1) PLUS humor: agreeableness reads as relief, the joke makes it charming. Contrast-with-combative-exes is the cover. She reads low-drama + funny. Residue (the resistance that goes underground instead of being voiced) dormant.

column5: Oh, you're ready on time for once. I'm kidding. You look incredible, relax.
column6: He teases me in this way that has a tiny sting but he's always immediately joking and complimentary right after, so I laugh it off. He's just playful, that's his sense of humor.
column7: Hostility-as-Deniable-Package: a dig at her ('on time for once') instantly wrapped in 'kidding' + a compliment, so the barb is deniable. The test is real, the joke is the cover. She laughs it off, blames nothing. Residue (faint accumulating stings she can't name) dormant. CONTRAST: Toby's humor never has a target; Blake's grazes her then retreats.

column5: I completely forgot to book it, I'm the worst. I'll sort it, promise. I owe you one.
column6: He forgets things sometimes but he's so funny and self-deprecating about it that I can't even be annoyed. He just charms his way out of it and I end up laughing.
column7: Inaction-as-Weapon (early/deniable, Inconsistency9): the dropped ball, but humor disarms any objection ('crimes against you'). The charm is the deflection mechanism. She can't stay annoyed. Residue (chronic let-down she's joked out of protesting) dormant. CONTRAST w/ Toby: Toby's forgetting comes with sincere repair; Blake's comes with a deflecting joke.

column5: No, I'll handle it, I always do. It's fine. Don't mind me.
column6: He jokes about being the one who handles everything, in this wry put-upon way. It's funny, but it also makes me feel a little bad, like maybe I should be doing more.
column7: Self-as-Put-Upon-Innocent (Accountability2) delivered as a JOKE so the guilt-induction is deniable; 'don't mind me' martyrdom wrapped in wit. She laughs AND feels the guilt-hook land. Residue (vague guilt, walking on eggshells under the comedy) dormant.

column6: Things are good, he's hilarious, everyone loves him. I can't totally explain it but I sometimes come away from a fun night feeling a little small and I can't put my finger on why. It's probably nothing, he's never actually mean, it's all jokes.
column7: Residue (honeymoon form of off-balance): the genuine fun affirmed AND the unnameable residue (feeling a little small after, can't pin why, 'it's all jokes'). She blames herself ('probably nothing'). Does NOT name it, the deniability via humor is the mechanism.

column5: Wow, you finished early? Look at you. I'm proud of you, genuinely. It was a joke, relax.
column6: He teases me in this way that has a tiny sting to it, but he's always joking and complimentary right after, so I just laugh it off. That's just his sense of humour, he doesn't mean anything by it.
column7: Hostility-as-Deniable-Package: a dig at her instantly wrapped in 'kidding' + a compliment, so the barb is deniable. The test is real, the joke is the cover. She laughs it off, blames nothing. CONTRAST: Toby's humor never targets her; Blake's grazes then retreats. Residue (faint accumulating stings) dormant.

column5: Honestly, whatever you want, I'm not fussy. You pick. I'm easy about this stuff.
column6: He's so easygoing and never turns anything into a fight, and he's funny about it too. After exes who argued about everything, being with someone this low-drama and lighthearted is such a relief.
column7: Compliance-as-Surface-Veneer (Conflict1) + humor: agreeableness reads as relief, the joke makes it charming. Contrast-with-combative-exes is the cover. She reads low-drama + funny. Residue (resistance goes underground instead of being voiced) dormant.

column5: I forgot to call the plumber, I know, I'm a disaster. In fairness I was thinking about you. I'll do it, probably today.
column6: He forgets to do things sometimes but he's so funny and self-deprecating about it that I can't be annoyed, he just charms his way out and I end up laughing instead of frustrated.
column7: Inaction-as-Weapon (early/deniable, Inconsistency9): the dropped task, but humor disarms the objection. The charm is the deflection. She can't stay annoyed. CONTRAST: Toby repairs sincerely; Blake deflects with a joke. Residue (chronic let-down she's joked out of protesting) dormant.

column5: No, you go enjoy your night, don't worry about me. I'll hold the fort, like always. Have fun.
column6: He jokes about being the one who holds everything together in this wry, put-upon way. It's funny, but it also leaves me feeling a little guilty, like maybe I should be doing more or staying in.
column7: Self-as-Put-Upon-Innocent (Accountability2) delivered as a JOKE so the guilt-induction is deniable; the martyrdom wrapped in wit. She laughs AND feels the guilt-hook land. Residue (vague guilt, eggshells under the comedy) dormant.

column7: SURFACE: distracted, not present, resembles Neglectful Nico. CAUSE: processing lag, conversation gets ahead of him. DISCRIMINATOR: asks explicitly for repetition, shows genuine desire to hear.

column5: I bought tickets to that thing you mentioned once. I don't remember why I walked into the kitchen but I remembered the tickets.
column6: He surprised me with tickets to a show I mentioned one time and I was so touched. He can't remember to take out the trash but he remembered that. His brain saves the wrong files.
column7: SURFACE: inconsistent attention, selfish prioritization, resembles Egocentric Evan. CAUSE: interest-based nervous system, not value judgment. DISCRIMINATOR: as confused by his own selectivity as she is.

column5: I forgot to call the landlord again. Can you stand next to me for two minutes while I do it? I hate that I need this.
column6: He asked me to babysit him through a phone call. He actually hates that he needs the help. I stood there, he made the call, and he thanked me like I'd done something huge.
column7: SURFACE: offloads basic tasks, weaponized incompetence, resembles Dependent Drew. CAUSE: task initiation deficit, body-doubling bridges the gap. DISCRIMINATOR: explicit discomfort with dependency, active gratitude, no assumption she owes this.

column5: I said yes to helping you move and yes to my friend's thing. I double-booked myself again. I just say yes to everything when it's in front of me.
column6: He double-booked on my moving day and I was furious. He wasn't choosing his friend over me, he just can't say no in the moment. I'm still moving boxes alone though.
column7: SURFACE: unreliable, breaks commitments, resembles partner who doesn't prioritize her. CAUSE: time blindness plus impulsivity, future conflicts don't feel real. DISCRIMINATOR: no minimization of her anger, genuine remorse.

column5: You reminded me three times and I still didn't do it. I'm not ignoring you. The urgency just falls off after five seconds.
column6: I reminded him about something important three times and he still forgot. The look on his face was genuine horror. He's not blowing me off, his urgency just has a short battery.
column7: SURFACE: ignores reminders, devalues her requests, resembles dismissive partner. CAUSE: urgency is momentary without ongoing cues. DISCRIMINATOR: no blame-shifting, no claim she's nagging, visible distress at his own failure.

column5: I saw you were upset earlier and I wanted to ask but then I forgot. I feel awful. Are you okay? Tell me now.
column6: He noticed I was upset hours ago and didn't say anything until now. He wasn't ignoring me on purpose, his brain just doesn't hold the intention to check in. I'm still hurt though.
column7: SURFACE: neglectful, doesn't attend to her, resembles Avoidant Alex. CAUSE: intention persistence fails without a cue. DISCRIMINATOR: no strategic withholding, genuine concern when he resurfaces, creates space for her now.

column5: I'm really excited about this new thing and I want to tell you everything. Tap my arm when I'm info-dumping. I won't notice I'm doing it.
column6: He talked at me for twenty minutes about his new hobby and I felt like furniture. Then he asked me to tap him. He wants to be reined in, he just can't feel when he's gone too far.
column7: SURFACE: self-absorbed, monologues, resembles Egocentric Evan. CAUSE: monotropic enthusiasm as connection, not admiration-seeking. DISCRIMINATOR: explicit invitation for a cue to stop, no expectation she should just listen.

column5: I put your art show in my phone and I still showed up an hour late, I'm so sorry, I genuinely thought I had loads of time and then I didn't.
column6: He missed the start of my art show even though he'd put it in his phone. He felt awful and I could tell it was real. It stung a little, but it's the first time, and he was so sorry.
column7: SURFACE: late to something that mattered to her, resembles Avoidant Alex's low investment. CAUSE: time-blindness, the calendar entry didn't translate into felt urgency. DISCRIMINATOR: honeymoon framing, single incident she forgives, genuine remorse, no defensiveness or pattern yet.

column5: I just spent an hour looking for my wallet. I found it in the fridge. I don't know how to explain that.
column6: He was late again because his wallet was in the fridge. I wanted to be furious but I was also baffled. He's not being careless on purpose, his brain just puts things down in random places.
column7: SURFACE: chaotic, disorganized, leaves her waiting, resembles partner who doesn't respect her time. CAUSE: working memory drift, actions decouple from intention. DISCRIMINATOR: no excuse-making, genuine confusion, chaos is global not aimed at her.

column5: You're telling me something important and I can feel my brain trying to leap ahead to what I'm going to say back. I'm going to fight it. I want to hear you.
column6: He told me he was fighting his own brain to listen. I didn't know whether to be touched or annoyed. I just wish he didn't have to fight so hard. But at least he's honest about it.
column7: SURFACE: distracted, listening as effortful, resembles Neglectful Nico. CAUSE: monotropic attention requires active suppression of parallel thoughts. DISCRIMINATOR: names the effort as his problem, goal is to hear her not perform listening.

column5: I am so sorry I'm late for our dinner, I got completely lost in a project at the workshop and looked up and it was already past eight. I set an alarm for next time, I swear.
column6: He showed up flustered and late to our dinner because he'd lost track of time at his workshop. The apology was so genuine I couldn't really stay annoyed. Hopefully the alarm thing helps.
column7: SURFACE: unreliable, late to a date, resembles Charming Charlie's casualness. CAUSE: hyperfocus plus time-blindness collapsed the hour. DISCRIMINATOR: early dating, one occasion not a tallied pattern, he proactively proposes a fix, remorse is sincere.

column5: I saw this vintage espresso machine on the way over and I just had to grab it, I know it was kind of impulsive, but imagine the coffee. I get like this when something cool grabs me.
column6: He turned up to our date carrying this random espresso machine he'd impulse-bought on the way. It was funny and a bit much at the same time. He's clearly someone who leaps before he looks, which is exciting but I clocked it.
column7: SURFACE: impulsive spending, slightly chaotic, resembles Adrenaline Ainsley's impulse-chasing. CAUSE: novelty-driven dopamine pull, motivation seized by the new object. DISCRIMINATOR: his own money and his own purchase, no shared finances, she finds it endearing but notices the impulsivity; cost is foreshadowed not incurred.

column5: Can you hold onto my wallet and keys while we're at this street fair? If they aren't physically zipped into your purse, I know they're going to drop right out of my head and vanish.
column6: We went to a festival and he immediately handed me his keys, wallet, and sunglasses to keep track of because he's afraid of losing them. I spent the whole afternoon worried about dropping his stuff on top of mine. It feels like I'm traveling with a kid instead of a partner, even though he asked nicely.
column7: SURFACE: helpless, treats her like a caretaker, offloads basic adulthood, resembles Dependent Drew. CAUSE: partner-as-place-things-are-kept frame to compensate for severe working-memory lapses. DISCRIMINATOR: he asks for help transparently and with zero entitlement or assumption that it is her duty, though the administrative burden still shifts onto her by default.

column5: You texted me to bring the book and I read it, pictured grabbing it, and then it just evaporated. I'm so sorry. If it's not literally in my hand I lose it.
column6: I asked him to bring a book he'd promised to lend me and he completely blanked, even though he'd read the text. He wasn't blowing me off, his brain just dropped it. A little annoying, but he was mortified.
column7: SURFACE: ignores her request, reads as not caring, resembles Neglectful Nico's low follow-through. CAUSE: out-of-sight-as-out-of-existence, the intention left memory once off-screen. DISCRIMINATOR: early dating, a borrowed book not a shared household task, genuine confusion and ownership, no minimizing.

column5: I know we said we'd just do a quiet dinner, but I just heard the old arcade downtown is closing tonight, come on, let's go right now, it'll be an adventure.
column6: We had a low-key night planned and he suddenly wanted to bolt across town to a closing arcade. His energy is genuinely fun, but I did notice my quiet-night idea just got steamrolled by his excitement. He wasn't pushy about it though, just lit up.
column7: SURFACE: overrides the plan, impulsive disruption, resembles a partner who disregards her preference. CAUSE: novelty-driven impulse, the fleeting opportunity seized him. DISCRIMINATOR: he invites warmly rather than demanding, early-stage, she's charmed but registers her plan got rolled over; no entitlement.

column5: Can you just tell me exactly what time I need to start getting ready for your family's brunch? If you don't give me the specific countdown, I'm going to get distracted by something and make us late.
column6: He asked me to manage his timeline for my parents' brunch so he wouldn't make us late. On one hand, I appreciate that he wants to be on time for my family, but on the other hand, I hate being his human alarm clock. It feels like I have to engineer his entire day just so he can show up for me.
column7: SURFACE: weaponized incompetence, forces her to manage his schedule, resembles Dependent Drew. CAUSE: partner-as-place-things-are-kept frame used to bridge an executive deficit in time tracking. DISCRIMINATOR: he recognizes his own limitation and explicitly asks for a scaffolding framework to protect her family's time, showing zero entitlement to her labor.

column5: I know I said I'd call the landlord on Tuesday, but that deadline felt like it was miles away when we talked about it. Suddenly it's Friday and the office is closed and I'm kicking myself for letting it slip.
column6: He promised he would call the landlord about our leak by Tuesday, and he just completely let it slide until the weekend. Now we're stuck with a wet bathroom for two more days. He's incredibly apologetic and mad at himself, but the real-world consequence falls on both of us. It's hard to trust his promises when time just evaporates on him.
column7: SURFACE: unreliable, neglects household safety, empty promises, resembles Nico. CAUSE: time-as-physical-distance frame where future tasks lack cognitive urgency until they arrive precipitously. DISCRIMINATOR: his remorse is authentic and directed inward as self-reproach rather than deflecting blame onto her or the landlord, though the logistical cost to her remains unmitigated.

column5: I'm so sorry I'm late, I lost my keys again, found them in my jacket in the end. My stuff just teleports when I'm not looking, I swear I'm not always this scattered.
column6: He was late picking me up because he'd lost his keys at home. He was sweet and embarrassed about it. It's a little scattered, but honestly kind of endearing this early on.
column7: SURFACE: disorganized, keeps her waiting, resembles a partner who doesn't respect her time. CAUSE: working-memory drift, objects decouple from where he set them. DISCRIMINATOR: single early incident, chaos is global and self-directed not aimed at her, she finds it endearing rather than draining.

column5: You looked away when I was telling you about my new guitar pedal, did I completely bore you? I feel like I'm being incredibly annoying right now and maybe I should just stop talking.
column6: He was sharing a story about his guitar stuff, and because I checked my watch for a second, he instantly shut down and asked if he was being annoying. It felt like I was walking on eggshells and had to constantly reassure him just for taking a breath. It's exhausting how fast his confidence bottoms out over nothing.
column7: SURFACE: fragile, demands constant validation, forces her to manage his emotional state, resembles Parker. CAUSE: rejection-sensitive dysphoria where a neutral shifting of attention is interpreted as social withdrawal or disapproval. DISCRIMINATOR: he is withdrawing to protect her from his perceived annoyingness rather than using a guilt-trip to force compliance or score points.

column5: I didn't finish cleaning the kitchen because the motivation just completely drained out of me halfway through the dishes. I was standing there and it felt like my brain went offline, so I had to leave the room.
column6: He left the kitchen half-cleaned with the water still in the sink because he said his brain went offline. It looked like pure, lazy defiance. I ended up finishing it because I couldn't stand the smell. He's not an aggressive guy, but the way he just drops tasks whenever he feels like it leaves me doing all the actual work of keeping this place livable.
column7: SURFACE: lazy, strategic incompetence, leaves chores for her, resembles Dependent Drew. CAUSE: engagement-as-a-place-you're-inside frame combined with an interest-based nervous system that stalls out on routine tasks. DISCRIMINATOR: there is no underlying stance of entitlement or belief that cleaning is her job; he is genuinely paralyzed by executive exhaustion, though the domestic deficit still defaults to her.

column5: I didn't answer your texts yesterday because I was completely buried under this engine breakdown at work and everything outside of that room just dropped out of my head. I didn't mean to make you worry, I'm here now.
column6: He went completely dark for twenty-four hours while he was fixing a machine at work. No 'I'm busy' text, nothing. I was convinced he'd crashed his car. When he finally messaged, he acted like no time had passed at all. I can't tell if he's completely emotionally detached or if he just lacks a basic sense of responsibility to his partner.
column7: SURFACE: intermittent reinforcement, goes dark to create distance, resembles Alex's avoidance. CAUSE: out-of-sight-as-out-of-existence frame driven by extreme monotropic hyperfocus on an immediate physical problem. DISCRIMINATOR: as soon as the physical constraint clears, he returns with immediate, unprompted warmth and full disclosure, completely free of tactical withholding or power-seeking.

column5: I am so incredibly sorry I forgot our six-month anniversary, I had it on my mental list but it just slipped down into the dark. Let's celebrate tonight instead, I'll cook your favorite pasta and we can start completely fresh.
column6: He completely forgot our six-month anniversary date. When I called him out, his face fell and he stayed up until midnight cooking this elaborate meal to make it up to me, swearing we were on a fresh slate. The food was amazing, but I'm realizing his intense romantic resets are just the other side of him completely spacing on me.
column7: SURFACE: hot and cold cycles, love-bombing after neglect, resembles Charming Charlie. CAUSE: apology-as-reset-button frame where intense affective empathy drives a dramatic repair attempt to clear the emotional debt. DISCRIMINATOR: his grand gestures are responses to genuine personal shame and a desire to connect, never a strategic cover to keep her off-balance or hide a pattern of exploitation.

column5: This is going to sound weird, but can you stay on the phone while I fill out this boring form? If someone's just there I can actually start it. You don't have to do anything.
column6: He asked me to stay on the phone while he did some boring paperwork, said he just can't start that stuff alone. It was a slightly odd ask this early, kind of sweet, kind of a glimpse of something. He was clear I didn't have to actually help.
column7: SURFACE: leans on her to function, foreshadows body-doubling dependence, resembles Dependent Drew. CAUSE: task-initiation deficit, presence as an anchor. DISCRIMINATOR: early dating and a phone call not a shared lease, he explicitly asks nothing of her labor, she notices the glimpse without being burdened.

column5: I was going to grab snacks for our movie night but the shop was so loud and bright I had to leave with nothing, I'm sorry, I'll order something in instead.
column6: He showed up to our movie night empty-handed because the shop got too overwhelming for him and he just left. It was a little flaky, but he seemed genuinely thrown by it and sorted dinner another way.
column7: SURFACE: drops a simple task, reads as unreliable, resembles Dependent Drew's incompetence. CAUSE: sensory overload in a loud bright environment. DISCRIMINATOR: snacks for a date not the household shop, no accumulated 'always the backup' framing, he distress is real and he finds an alternative himself.

column5: I said I'd help you carry those boxes up today and I totally meant it, then I got buried sorting my records instead. I feel terrible, let me come over tonight and do it.
column6: He'd offered to help me move some boxes and then got lost reorganizing his record collection instead. Classic. He felt bad and offered to come fix it, but I'd half-expected to do it alone by then.
column7: SURFACE: bails on a promise, resembles a partner who doesn't follow through. CAUSE: a high-friction task stalled while a high-interest one grabbed him. DISCRIMINATOR: early-stage favor not a shared chore list, he offers genuine repair, the cost is a single missed offer she shrugs off.

column5: I am telling you every single detail about the orbital trajectory of the Apollo missions because I've been reading about it all night and it's the coolest thing I've ever discovered. I know you're trying to read your book, but you have to hear this one part.
column6: I was trying to decompress with my book after a long day and he just steamrolled right over me, talking for an hour about rocket trajectories. He was so loud and excited that I couldn't even focus on my own page. It felt incredibly selfish, like my need for quiet was completely invisible to his desire to hear himself talk.
column7: SURFACE: self-centered, tramples her boundaries, dominates the room, resembles Evan's grandiosity. CAUSE: intense interest-based nervous system driving an uninhibited social bid to share high-dopamine data as connection. DISCRIMINATOR: he seeks shared enthusiasm rather than personal admiration or superiority; his boundaries lapse due to excitement rather than a belief that her time is less valuable.

column5: You asked me last week if I wanted to come to your friend's party and I never answered, did I? It just slipped right out of my head, I'm sorry, yes, I'd love to come.
column6: He never got back to me about whether he'd come to my friend's party, then suddenly remembered a week later. Not on purpose, his brain just lost it. A little frustrating, but he was keen once he remembered.
column7: SURFACE: leaves her hanging on a social invite, resembles Neglectful Nico's low engagement. CAUSE: out-of-sight working-memory omission. DISCRIMINATOR: a casual party invite early on not a locked wedding headcount, he owns it and is enthusiastic, no defensiveness.

column5: I am so sorry I snapped at you when you asked about the electric bill, I was in the middle of a massive work crunch and my brain just sparked out from the interruption. Let's completely wipe the slate clean, I'll pay the bill right now and buy us dinner.
column6: I asked him a quick question about a bill while he was typing, and he barked at me to shut up. Ten minutes later, he came out of his room with a huge apology, paid the bill, and ordered my favorite takeout, begging for a total reset. The dinner was nice, but the whiplash from being yelled at to being courted within thirty minutes is dizzying.
column7: SURFACE: verbal volatility, quick to anger, uses gifts to smooth over outbursts, resembles Marley. CAUSE: verbal impulsivity and poor emotional inhibition when interrupted during an intense hyperfocus state, followed by immediate RSD-driven shame. DISCRIMINATOR: he doesn't justify his anger, blame her timing, or make her earn his good mood back; he takes full ownership and moves immediately to concrete behavioral repair.

column5: I swear I was about to leave, then I couldn't find my keys, then my wallet, then somehow I was cleaning the junk drawer. I'm so sorry, I know your time mattered.
column6: He was forty minutes late and showed up sweaty and genuinely upset, which made it hard to stay mad. But I was sitting there alone again, and the apology doesn't give me those forty minutes back.
column7: SURFACE: chronic lateness that reads as devaluing her, resembles Neglectful Nico's disregard. CAUSE: time-as-physical-distance plus object loss derailing the sequence. DISCRIMINATOR: he owns the impact without blaming her, but the cost to her is still real.

column5: I interrupted because the thought was leaving. That's not an excuse. I wrote it down now, go back, I want to hear the rest.
column6: He cuts me off constantly, and it makes me feel like my stories are just launchpads for his brain. Then he catches himself and tries, but I am tired of having to fight for the end of my own sentences.
column7: SURFACE: self-absorbed conversational dominance, resembles Egocentric Evan. CAUSE: working-memory urgency and thought-loss anxiety. DISCRIMINATOR: he redirects attention back to her and uses a system, not superiority, though the interruption still hurts.

column5: I said I'd help with your proposal and I meant it. Then the app idea grabbed my whole brain and I lost the proposal completely.
column6: He was so excited about helping me that I actually counted on it. Then some new idea ate his entire weekend and I ended up doing the proposal alone. I believe he meant it, which almost makes it more confusing.
column7: SURFACE: hot-cold support and broken promise, resembles Avoidant Alex or Storytelling Sam. CAUSE: motivation-as-force-from-task, attention gets captured by the new salient thing. DISCRIMINATOR: the failure is not strategic future-faking, but the unreliability still harms her.

column5: I forgot dinner with your sister. I put it in the calendar wrong. That's on me, not you, not the app, me.
column6: He forgot my sister's dinner after I reminded him twice. He didn't blame me, which is something, but I still had to smooth it over with her and sit there embarrassed.
column7: SURFACE: careless disregard for her family, resembles Neglectful Nico's low investment. CAUSE: time and calendar encoding failure. DISCRIMINATOR: he accepts responsibility without DARVO, but she still carries the social fallout.

column5: I got excited and offered too much. I wanted to be that guy. I am not that guy yet.
column6: He promised to cook, pick up the package, and help me clean, then did maybe one and a half things. I appreciate the honesty, but I'm so tired of dating the gap between his intentions and what actually happens.
column7: SURFACE: unreliable overpromising, resembles Storytelling Sam's promise-replacing-behavior. CAUSE: enthusiasm outruns goal persistence and planning capacity. DISCRIMINATOR: he admits the gap directly rather than selling a bigger future, but the gap remains a real burden.

column5: When you said 'it's fine' like that, my stomach dropped. I know you didn't attack me. My brain reacted like you did.
column6: I said it was fine because I was trying not to make a thing of it, and he looked like I'd punched him. Then I had to pause my own irritation because he was spiraling. He knew it wasn't fair, but it still took over the room.
column7: SURFACE: disproportionate hurt reaction, resembles Pity-Party Parker or Menacing Marley. CAUSE: rejection-sensitive dysphoria triggered by perceived disapproval. DISCRIMINATOR: he names it as his reaction and does not accuse her, but the emotional labor cost is real.

column5: I opened your message at work, answered it in my head, then it vanished from the universe. I hate that I do this.
column6: He left me on read all day and I felt pathetic checking my phone. He really did think he'd answered, which is almost worse because how am I supposed to compete with his brain erasing me?
column7: SURFACE: neglectful silence or avoidant distance, resembles Avoidant Alex. CAUSE: out-of-sight-as-out-of-existence and working-memory lapse. DISCRIMINATOR: no strategic withdrawal, but the felt abandonment is legitimate.

column5: I need you to sit here while I start the tax form. Don't do it for me, just be the anchor.
column6: He asked me to sit beside him so he could start his taxes. It's sweet that he doesn't want me to do it for him, but also why am I the human paperweight for basic adult tasks?
column7: SURFACE: dependency and parentification, resembles Dependent Drew. CAUSE: engagement-as-place-inside, body-doubling helps him enter the task. DISCRIMINATOR: he explicitly says not to do it for him, but still pulls her into executive support.

column5: I know I said clean slate yesterday. I meant it emotionally, but the dishes are still real. I'm doing them now.
column6: He does this thing where he apologizes so sincerely that I feel like we're starting over, but then the same pile is still there. This time he actually got up and did it, but I'm watching to see if that lasts.
column7: SURFACE: serial apology with recurring pattern, resembles Parker's remorse loop. CAUSE: apology-as-reset-button, remorse creates a felt reset before systems change. DISCRIMINATOR: he connects apology to action here, but recurrence is a real risk.

column5: I texted you sixteen thoughts at 2 a.m. because they all connected to your idea. Then I crashed and missed your reply.
column6: He sent this huge excited essay about my project in the middle of the night, then disappeared the next day. It felt weirdly both supportive and totally unavailable. I don't know what to do with that.
column7: SURFACE: intense pursuit then drop-off, resembles Charming Charlie or Avoidant Alex. CAUSE: dopamine-driven associative engagement followed by depletion. DISCRIMINATOR: the content is genuinely about her idea, not a seduction script, but the inconsistency still disorients her.

column5: I lost the thread halfway through your story, and I panicked because I care about the story. Start from the part with your boss again.
column6: He admitted he stopped following while I was talking, which hurt. But he asked me to restart because he cared, not because he was bored. Still, I hate having to rewind myself like a podcast.
column7: SURFACE: inattentive listening, resembles Neglectful Nico's emotional absence. CAUSE: attention drift and thread loss. DISCRIMINATOR: he asks to re-engage instead of pretending or dismissing, but the burden shifts to her to repeat.

column5: Wait, the show is tonight? I had it in my head as next Friday. Okay, give me twenty minutes, I'm coming, I really want to be there.
column6: He nearly missed the gig we'd been excited about because he had the date wrong in his head. He scrambled to make it and did. I was a bit deflated at first, but he genuinely wanted to be there.
column7: SURFACE: nearly misses an important plan, resembles low-care neglect. CAUSE: time-as-physical-distance, the date sat wrong in his mind. DISCRIMINATOR: he mobilizes immediately and shows up, early-stage excitement intact, her disappointment is mild and resolves.

column5: I know the laundry exploded because I started sorting by fabric, then color, then remembered the closet shelves. I see the problem.
column6: He turned laundry into an entire apartment disaster. He genuinely saw the problem after I pointed at it, but I am the one living inside all these unfinished explosions.
column7: SURFACE: chaotic mess left for her, resembles Dependent Drew offloading domestic labor. CAUSE: task-branching and poor goal persistence. DISCRIMINATOR: he is not comfortable assigning her cleanup, but his unfinished loops still load her environment.

column5: I reacted like your feedback meant I failed as a person. It didn't. I need ten minutes, then I'll listen properly.
column6: I told him the plan wasn't working and he looked devastated, like I'd called him useless. At least he didn't attack me, but I still had to wait for the emotional storm to pass before we could solve the actual issue.
column7: SURFACE: volatile response to feedback, resembles Marley or Parker. CAUSE: rejection-sensitive dysphoria with shame spike. DISCRIMINATOR: he contains it, asks for time, and returns to listen rather than punishing her.

column5: I said yes to brunch because I wanted to want it. Morning came and my brain wouldn't enter the task.
column6: He agreed to brunch and then couldn't get out the door. I know he wasn't trying to humiliate me, but I still had to cancel and look flaky because of him.
column7: SURFACE: unreliable cancellation, resembles Avoidant Alex's withdrawal. CAUSE: engagement-as-place-inside, intention does not guarantee task initiation. DISCRIMINATOR: the failure is not relational avoidance, but the social cost falls on her.

column5: I put your birthday gift somewhere safe, which means it has entered the void. I am fixing this today.
column6: He lost my birthday gift before giving it to me. I wish that were a joke. He looked horrified and went back out to replace it, but I still felt weirdly forgotten on my own birthday.
column7: SURFACE: careless neglect on a meaningful occasion, resembles Nico. CAUSE: out-of-sight-as-out-of-existence and object misplacement. DISCRIMINATOR: he repairs proactively, but her hurt is still valid.

column5: Your idea is amazing, I can help build it this weekend. Wait, write that down for me or I'll lose it.
column6: He was so excited about my idea that I felt supported for ten minutes, then he asked me to write down his offer so he wouldn't forget. It made me laugh, then made me tired.
column7: SURFACE: makes her manage his support, resembles Dependent Drew. CAUSE: partner-as-place-things-are-kept, external memory reliance. DISCRIMINATOR: the offer is genuine and about her, but he immediately shifts tracking labor onto her.

column5: I missed the exit because I was explaining the podcast. That's my fault. Please don't let me navigate and talk.
column6: He drove us twenty minutes the wrong way because he got excited about a podcast. He owned it, but I still ended up hungry and late and silently assigning myself the job of navigator forever.
column7: SURFACE: careless disruption and incompetence, resembles Drew. CAUSE: single-channel attention and excitement-driven distraction. DISCRIMINATOR: he proposes a concrete system, but the adaptation may still become her job.

column5: I know I said I'd call after dinner. Then the new song loop swallowed three hours. I hate that you waited.
column6: He forgot to call because he got obsessed with a song. That sounds ridiculous, but he was genuinely upset that I waited. Still, I waited. That's the part I can't keep brushing off.
column7: SURFACE: hot-cold neglect, resembles Avoidant Alex. CAUSE: engagement-as-place-inside and time collapse during hyperfocus. DISCRIMINATOR: remorse centers her wait, not his excuse, but repeated waiting remains harm.

column5: I don't want you to be my manager. I also don't know how to hold all these moving parts yet.
column6: This is the thing. He knows I shouldn't be managing him. He doesn't want that. But somehow I still know the dentist, the rent, the groceries, the birthdays, and where his passport is.
column7: SURFACE: partner becomes manager, resembles Dependent Drew. CAUSE: partner-as-place-things-are-kept plus executive load collapse. DISCRIMINATOR: he explicitly rejects entitlement to her labor, but the imbalance persists.

column5: I blurted that your friend talks forever because the thought came out before the manners arrived. I need to apologize to her.
column6: He said my friend talks forever, to her face. I wanted to disappear. He was immediately horrified and apologized without making excuses, but I spent the rest of the night tense.
column7: SURFACE: rude, socially careless, resembles Backhanded Blake. CAUSE: impulsive speech before social filtering. DISCRIMINATOR: no cutting intent and immediate repair, but social fallout is real.

column5: I can't start cleaning alone. If you sit on the couch and talk at me, I can start moving.
column6: I don't want to be his mother, but somehow my presence is the button that makes him start chores. It works, which is almost more annoying because now I know how to get it done.
column7: SURFACE: offloading domestic initiation, resembles Dependent Drew. CAUSE: body-doubling helps task entry. DISCRIMINATOR: he is not asking her to do the chore, but her labor becomes the activation system.

column5: I thought I confirmed the reservation. I confirmed it in my head, which apparently does not count as reality.
column6: He didn't book the restaurant for my promotion dinner because he thought about booking it and somehow that became the same as booking it. He was mortified. I was still standing outside in heels with no table.
column7: SURFACE: careless failure around her milestone, resembles Nico's neglect. CAUSE: intention-action confusion and working-memory failure. DISCRIMINATOR: mortification and ownership are real, but they do not erase the missed celebration.

column5: I got scared when you sounded disappointed and my brain went straight to 'she's done.' I know that's not fair to you.

CONTRAST: a predator violates boundaries because he feels entitled and correction yields performed contrition then repetition (erosion IS the goal, Dominance10/Exploitation10); Bailey violated from not-perceiving, is truly ashamed, and STOPS for good. Correction works permanently. 'Sorry meant until next time' vs 'corrected once means corrected for good' is the whole tell.
